IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of low-resource paradigm completion (noting that only ≈200 of ~7000 languages have annotations) and demonstrates substantial practical gains (up to 58% higher accuracy) using cross-lingual encoder–decoder transfer across 21 language pairs. It also establishes zero-/one-shot capabilities and provides analyses (e.g., ciphering experiments) that separate true linguistic transfer from mere regularization, showing the central role of language relatedness. These results are likely to change how researchers approach morphological transfer and to spur follow-up work on multilingual/typology-aware transfer for low-resource NLP, though they are incremental rather than fully transformative.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper addresses cross-lingual transfer for paradigm completion, a core NLP problem in morphology, using state-of-the-art encoder–decoder RNNs with attention (Sec. 3.1) and frames transfer as multi-task learning — well within top NLP/ML venue scope. It targets the right audience by evaluating low-resource, one-/zero-shot transfer across 21 language pairs from multiple families and comparing baselines (e.g., ciphering experiment and Arabic baseline), matching community evaluation standards (SIGMORPHON cited, extensive multilingual experiments). The formulation, methods and empirical setup are standard for leading conferences and the work is positioned against relevant prior work in MT and morphological reinflection, making it a strong fit.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is overall well-organized (intro, model, experiments, discussion) and the main method is presented early (Sections 3–3.1) with clear equations (Eqs. 2–5) and a useful Figure 1 illustrating the encoder–decoder. However, the manuscript contains many repeated/mangled passages and citation artifacts (e.g., multiple verbatim repeats of the same sentences, corrupted reference sequences and stray commas) that make parts of the text hard to follow and interrupt the flow. Notation is mostly consistent and defined where used, and examples (the Spanish/Portuguese discussion, the ciphering experiment) are informative, but the presentation would benefit from thorough copyediting to remove duplications and LaTeX/reference glitches.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies existing encoder–decoder RNNs with attention to a new cross-lingual, multi-task setup for paradigm completion and explicitly trains a single multilingual model by prepending language tags. While the task focus (low-resource paradigm completion) and the comprehensive empirical analysis across 21 language pairs (including the cipher experiment to tease apart true transfer vs. regularization) are useful and novel in this application, the core modeling idea closely follows prior seq2seq and multilingual MT work (and a very similar MT approach was developed in parallel (Ha et al.)). Overall this represents an incremental but solid extension rather than a fundamentally new method.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments are broad and multilingual (21 language pairs across Romance, Slavic, Uralic families with an Arabic baseline) and include useful analyses such as varying low-resource sample sizes (e.g. 50 vs 200), multi-source setups, and the ciphering experiment specifically designed to separate true linguistic transfer from a regularization effect. The paper also discusses limitations (transfer is effective mainly for related languages; Arabic acting as a regularizer) and situates the work thoroughly in prior work (many concrete comparisons). Missing, however, are standard architectural ablations and fuller reproducibility detail (exact model sizes, training epochs/early-stopping, seeds, preprocessing/code release), so while the empirical coverage is strong there are minor gaps that prevent full replication.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states its main claims clearly (cross-lingual encoder–decoder with tied parameters enables one-/zero-shot paradigm completion and up to 58% accuracy gains) and supports them with experiments across multiple families and a targeted ciphering ablation to separate true transfer from regularization (see Sec. 5.3). The mathematical formulation (joint log-likelihood Eq.2 and attention-based decoder Eqs.4–5) is standard and correct, and the experimental setup includes sensible baselines (no-transfer and unrelated-language Arabic baseline). However, the provided text omits crucial experimental details (dataset sizes per condition, exact metrics and significance testing) and relies on a conjecture linking lexical similarity to transferability without fully quantified analysis in the excerpt, so some experimental-reporting gaps remain.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against sensible baselines: a no-transfer baseline and an unrelated-language (Arabic) baseline, and performs a careful experiment (ciphering) to separate true linguistic transfer from regularization (see Exp. 3 discussion). It cites and positions itself relative to state-of-the-art monolingual encoder–decoder models and SIGMORPHON results and discusses methodological differences to prior multitask/MT transfer work. However, it does not present direct empirical comparisons to other prior paradigm-completion or cross-lingual transfer methods (e.g., Durrett & DeNero, Nicolai et al.) on the same evaluation, so some relevant baselines are omitted.
